coredata.dc:title: A low-cost hybrid GQDs/Fe3O4/polypyrrole nanocomposite based chemo-sensor for electrochemical non-enzymatic selective determination of creatinine in biological samples
coredata.dc:creator: Bhuvaneswari, Chandran
Elangovan, Arumugam
Sudhan, Narayanan
Vinodhkumar, Ganesan
Saravanan, Soorangkattan
Balasubramanian, Venkataraman
Sharmila, Chandrasekaran
Karuppaiah, Sudha
coredata.dc:description: A novel electrochemical non-enzymatic chemo-sensor based on low-cost hybrid graphene quantum dots/iron oxide/polypyrrole nanocomposite (GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy NC) was synthesised by solvothermal method to determine the creatinine (CTN). The physicochemical properties of prepared nanocomposite have been studied by XRD, XPS, FTIR spectroscopy, UV–visible spectroscopy, FE-SEM, EDX and HRTEM analysis. Herein, glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was modified with GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy NC and the voltametric sensing behaviour of CTN was analysed by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) techniques. The synergetic coupling effect of Fe3O4 nanoparticles with GQDs and Ppy in nanocomposites may provide an excellent catalytic activity towards CTN sensing, which exhibits a good wide linear range (0.1 to 100 µM) with the lowest detection limits (0.009 µM). Moreover, it is a good sensor platform with high selectivity towards CTN in the existence of significant interference such as ascorbic acid, dopamine, uric acid, urea, L-cysteine, glucose and heavy inorganic metals like cadmium and lead. Additionally, it displays better repeatability, reproducibility and sensitivity. Besides, Real-time feasibility of the developed sensor device was successfully utilized for the determination of CTN being proved in actual human blood serum and urine samples with and without the addition of commercial synthetic samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene Quantum Dots
GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy
Creatinine
Electrochemical sensor
Non-enzymatic

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electrochemical activity of GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy modified GCE
Electrochemical investigation of CTN at GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy/GCE
Determination of CTN at GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy modified GCE by DPV techniques
Reproducibility, repeatability, cyclic stability and storage capability of developed sensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India
University Grants Commission - South Eastern Regional Office

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Dr. A. E expresses gratitude to The Management of Thiagarajar College, and DST, Govt of India, (DST-SB/EMEQ)- 215/2013, UGC, Govt of India (UGC-MRP 6451/16(SERO/UGC)) for their financial support. Dr. AE thanks and is grateful to the Madurai Kidney Centre for providing Human blood serum and Urine samples for Creatinine analysis.

Dr. A. E expresses gratitude to The Management of Thiagarajar College, and DST, Govt of India, (DST-SB/EMEQ)- 215/2013, UGC, Govt of India (UGC-MRP 6451/16(SERO/UGC)) for their financial support. Dr. AE thanks and is grateful to the Madurai Kidney Centre for providing Human blood serum and Urine samples for Creatinine analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD of (a) Ppy, (b) Fe3O4 NPs, (c) GQD/Fe3O4 and (d) GQD/Fe3O4/Ppy NC.
FTIR spectra of Ppy (a), Fe3O4 NPs (b), GQD/Fe3O4 (c) and GQD/Fe3O4/Ppy NC (d).
UV–Vis absorption spectra of Ppy (black), Fe3O4 NPs (Red), GQD/Fe3O4 (blue) and GQD/Fe3O4/Ppy NC (pink).
FE-SEM micrographs of (a) bare Ppy, (b) pristine Fe3O4 NPs, (c) GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy NC, (d-f) EDX spectra of Ppy, Fe3O4NPs and GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy NC.
Elemental mapping of (a) Ppy, (b) pristine Fe3O4 NPs, (c) GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy NC.
HRTEM images of (A-D) GQDs at different magnifications and (E) SAED pattern of GQDs.
Complete XPS survey spectrum of GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy NC (A). The high resolution XPS spectra for Fe 2p (B), O 1s (C), and C 1s(D).

(A) Electrochemical impedance spectra recorded in 1 M KCl solution containing 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− at bare GCE, GQDs/GCE, Fe3O4/GCE, Ppy/GCE and GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy/GCE. (B) CV curve for GCE, GQDs/GCE, Fe3O4/GCE, Ppy/GCE and GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy modified GCE in 1 M KCl solution containing 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. (C) CV curve of GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy/GCE in 1 M KCl solution containing 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−solution at different scan rates from 10 to 200 mV/s. (D) corresponding linear plot of anodic and cathodic peak current vs. different scan rate.

(A) CVs of 50 µM CTN in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) at bare GCE, GQDs/GCE, Fe3O4/GCE, Ppy/GCE, GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy/GCE recorded at the scan rate 50 mV/s. (B) CV of GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy/GCE in the presence of 50 µM CTN containing 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) with different scan rates from 100 to 500 mV/s (Inset) Linear plot for redox peak current vs. different scan rate. (C) CV curve for different pH (3 to 11) in the presence 50 µM of CTN on modified GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy/GCE at 50 mV/s of scan rate. (D) CV curve for optimization of peak current in physiological pH (7.2 to 7.8) in the presence of 50 µM of CTN at GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy modified GCE at the scan rate 50 mV/s. (E) calibration plot for different pH vs. peak current.

(A) DPV were recorded for various concentrations of CTN ranging from 0.1 to 100 µM in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) at GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy/GCE. (B) Corresponding linear calibration plot. (C) DPV signal of CTN in the presence of co-interfering compounds (inset: corresponding bar diagram for the interfering species vs. peak current.
DPV curve for human blood serum and urine samples with spiking (A&B) and without spiked samples (C&D).
Schematic illustration of synthesize and fabrication of GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy NC towards CTN sensing.
Comparison of CTN using different modified GCE.

head.ce:title: A low-cost hybrid GQDs/Fe3O4/polypyrrole nanocomposite based chemo-sensor for electrochemical non-enzymatic selective determination of creatinine in biological samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: PG & Research Department of Chemistry, Thiagarajar College, Madurai-09, affiliated to Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai-21, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: PG& Research Department of Chemistry, Thiagarajar College, Madurai-09, affiliated to Madurai Kamaraj University, Madurai-21, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: PG & Research Department of Physics, Raja Doraisingam Government Arts College, Sivagangai 630561, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: PG& Research Department of Physics, Raja Doraisingam Government Arts College, Sivagangai 630561, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: PG & Research Department of Botany, The Madura College, Madurai-625011, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Botany, The Madura College, Madurai-625011, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Sona College of Technology, Salem 636005, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, Sona College of Technology, Salem 636005, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of synthesize and fabrication of GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy NCs towards CTN sensing.

A novel electrochemical non-enzymatic chemo-sensor based on low-cost hybrid graphene quantum dots/iron oxide/polypyrrole nanocomposite (GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy NC) was synthesised by solvothermal method to determine the creatinine (CTN). The physicochemical properties of prepared nanocomposite have been studied by XRD, XPS, FTIR spectroscopy, UV–visible spectroscopy, FE-SEM, EDX and HRTEM analysis. Herein, glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was modified with GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy NC and the voltametric sensing behaviour of CTN was analysed by cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) techniques. The synergetic coupling effect of Fe3O4 nanoparticles with GQDs and Ppy in nanocomposites may provide an excellent catalytic activity towards CTN sensing, which exhibits a good wide linear range (0.1 to 100 µM) with the lowest detection limits (0.009 µM). Moreover, it is a good sensor platform with high selectivity towards CTN in the existence of significant interference such as ascorbic acid, dopamine, uric acid, urea, L-cysteine, glucose and heavy inorganic metals like cadmium and lead. Additionally, it displays better repeatability, reproducibility and sensitivity. Besides, Real-time feasibility of the developed sensor device was successfully utilized for the determination of CTN being proved in actual human blood serum and urine samples with and without the addition of commercial synthetic samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In the recent years, chronic kidney disorder has become more prevalent, which continues to inevitably rise in clinical diagnostics due to its related mortality rate of 41.5 % during the past few decades. Metabolised CTN obtained from creatine (CTN, 2-Amino-1-methyl-5H-imidazole-4-one) was subsequently migrated via the bloodstream to renal kidneys and finally emitted through urine. As a result, the aggregating signal of CTN is a key biomarker of renal function evaluation. The average normal range of CTN in both serum and urine is 53–106 µM (0.6–1.2 mg dL−1) and 2.5–23 mM (37–300 mg dL−1), respectively. The presence of a high level of CTN will cause chronic kidney disorder, which will increases the consumption of protein meanwhile, a low-level CTN also gives some adverse effects like anaemia, inflammatory muscle disease, advanced renal disease, renal stenosis etc [1–5]. Therefore, the detection of CTN is necessary in human urine and blood serum samples for clinical surveillance. Numerous established copious methods such as high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), capillary electrophoresis (CE), Raman, GCMS and electrochemical methods are used to detect the CTN. Among these techniques, electrochemical method was chosen owing to its huge compensation, such as low cost, high attractiveness, time consumption, robustness, portability and easy-to-handle [6–10].

Recently, several emerging carbon-based nanomaterials, such as single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNT), carbon nanodots, graphene oxide and graphene quantum dots (GQDs), have been introduced into the carbon-based nanomaterial family, which are fascinating to many researchers [11–16]. GQDs are a zero dimensional material, derivative of graphene, which has potential interest in various applications due to their unique opto-electronic property and fluorescent property with adaptable band gap energy (2.2 to 3.1 eV) [17,18]. Moreover, the GQDs were the right adaptable material in electro-catalytic research. However, their catalytic activity is relatively slow to rectify that problem which is based on the active combination of GQDs with other materials to enhance the sensitivity of GQDs in sensor applications [19–21]. Fe3O4 (magnetite) is one of the most extensively used materials for diverse biological applications which are attracted much attention in the recent years due to their nontoxic, biocompatibility, unique paramagnetic nature, high absorptive, opto-electronics and electrical properties [22–25]. Mainly, Fe3O4 has a biodegradable property which oxidative ionic states of Fe3O4 layup in body reservoirs and are eventually taken up by erythrocytes as haemoglobin. The crystal lattice arrangement of Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Fe3O4 NPs) consisted of central metal Fe ions located at interstitial sites in their crystal structure, which is surrounded by oxygen ions in their closely packed cubic lattice. The electrical conductivity of Fe3O4 immensely arises due to the electrons leap between the ferrous (Fe2+) and ferric (Fe3+) ions in the crystal system [26–31]. Meanwhile, a large rate of electron charge carrier aggregation of pristine Fe3O4 falls out of their active, reactive sites, which impact on the effectiveness of the sensing target species [32]. At this juncture, to overcome the enhancement of electro-catalytic activity of both Fe3O4 and GQDs was resolved through synthesis of Fe3O4/GQDs with conducting polymer. Polypyrrole (Ppy) has been successfully applied in previous years for the electrochemical sensor application due to large surface area, reaction ability, heterocyclic structure, nontoxic, ease of synthesis and more conductivity. Moreover, it is capable of integrating with metal oxides and graphene based material as an ancestor to enhance the analytical performance when compared with solely GQDs or Ppy or Fe3O4 NPs, which can occur due to one of main potential reasons such that the redox nature of α- or β-C atoms was accelerated in the rings of Ppy through GQDs. It can hinder the diffusion and migration process and to sustain the synergistic effect between nanocomposites. Therefore, in this work, Fe3O4 and GQDs are usually incorporated with conducting polymers to avert oxidation, agglomeration, and for further functionalization which lead to help the efficient catalytic support in electrochemical fuel cells, drug delivery, sensors, and battery applications [33–35].

Hence, the present research work strongly reports synthesis of GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy NC by solvothermal method and sonication method to detect important potential biomarker CTN in the human urine and blood serum samples (Scheme 1 ). The newly designed sensor platform confirms and strongly supports onsite investigations of CTN for clinical applications.

CTN, starch, FeCl3·6H2O, ethylene glycol, sodium acetate anhydrous, pyrrole, ethylenediamine, methyl orange and ethanol were acquired from Sigma Aldrich. The essential chemical components are directly used without any further refinement. Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4 chemical compounds were used to prepare phosphate buffer solution (PBS) (Merck, Mumbai) and to adjust the pH (7.0) of the PBS by adding 0.1 M NaOH and HCl. All necessary chemicals were of analytical grade, and deionized water (DI) was used to prepare the required solutions.

The electrochemical characterization was carried out by using CHI electrochemical workstation (CHI 660E, Austin, TX-USA model). A three-electrode electrochemical cell was used throughout the electrochemical investigation. The working electrode was modified with GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy NC, while the Ag/AgCl was kept as the reference electrode, and the auxiliary electrode was platinum wire. The differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) techniques were used for the electrochemical detection of CTN over the wide potential ranges of −0.6 to 0.6 V at 50 mV/s scan rate under PBS. To determine the phase purity formation of the prepared nanocomposite, X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed in XPERT-PRO diffractometer with a source of Cu Kα radiation (K = 1.5406 Å) at 40 kV. Surface morphology of prepared GQDs was analysed by high-resolution transmission electron spectroscopy (HRTEM) Jeol/JEM model 2100. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, ZEISS Sigma 300 microscope), and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was utilized to investigate the elemental composition and morphology of synthesised nanocomposite. Furthermore, Fourier transform infra-red (FTIR-JASCO, Japan, Model 460 plus) spectra was recorded in the range of 4000 cm−1 to 400 cm−1 to analyse the functional group present in nanocomposite.

Initially, 0.5 g of starch was dissolute in 80 mL of DI water and continuously stirred for 30 min at 60 °C to gelatinize the starch. Then the mixture was poured into the 100 mL Teflon-lined reactor and treated at 180 °C for 7 h and then allowed to cool at room temperature. The obtained black solution was filtered through a filter paper and, the product was called as GQDs.

GQDs/Fe3O4 nanocomposite (GQDs/Fe3O4)NC was prepared by solvothermal deposition. In this experiment, 0.075 g of FeCl3·6H2O was dissolved in 60 mL ethylene glycol and kept for ultrasonication to form a clear solution. Afterwards, 0.225 g of sodium acetate anhydrous was added to 10 mL of ethylene glycol under continuous stirring for 30 min to form a clear solution. Then the sodium acetate solution was mixed with FeCl3 solution under constant stirring, followed by the addition of 2.5 mL of ethylenediamine (EDA) in order to improve the uniformity of the Fe3O4 NPs and continued stirring process for another 30 min to obtain a homogenous solution. Then, 10 mL volume of GQDs solution was added drop by drop to the above solution. The obtained solution was transferred into a 100 mL of autoclave and treated at around 180 °C for 10 h. Subsequently, the black precipitate was yielded by the centrifugation and washed with water and ethanol, then dried in a hot air oven at 70 °C for 24 h by solvothermal method.

Initially, 50 mg of GQDs/Fe3O4 NC was dissolved in 50 mL of 0.1 M HCl and then ultrasonicated for 30 min. To this solution, 1 M monomer pyrrole was added drop wise by maintaining a temperature below 10 °C. To this above suspension, 0.1 M of FeCl3 solution containing 5 mM methyl orange was added drop wise to the above mixture to initiate the chemical oxidative polymerization of pyrrole. Then, the mixture was continuously stirred at 5–10 °C for 2 hrs and left under static conditions for 24 h at 5 °C. The black precipitated was collected by filtration, washed with ethanol several times, and finally dried at 60 °C for 12 h. For comparison, pristine Ppy was prepared by a similar method.

To attain a clean surface, the GCE (3 mm in diameter) was polished with alumina powder (0.05 µm), thoroughly washed with DI water and then washed with ethanol. In the meantime, a uniform suspension was obtained through ultrasonically blending the as-prepared GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy NC for 20 min in a bath-sonicator at a concentration of 5 mg/mL. As a result, a 10 µL of GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy suspension was drop caste on the GCE surface and modified GCE was used for electrochemical investigation after being dried in a hot air oven for a few min.

The crystalline phase of above synthesized nanocomposite was characterized with a XPERT-PRO Diffractometer fitted with Cu kα (λ = 0.15406 nm) radiation 40 kV and 2θ range of 10° to 80°. Fig. 1 displayed the XRD patterns of Ppy, Fe3O4 NPs, GQD/Fe3O4 NC and GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy NC. Fig. 1(a) shows the wide peak observed at 2θ ranges from 20° to 30°. This result implies that Ppy was amorphous because of the scattering at the interplanar spacing of the polymeric chains. The XRD pattern of Fe3O4 NPs and GQD/Fe3O4 NC was depicted in Fig. 1(b) &(c). The sharp diffracted peaks appeared at 2θ = 30.0°, 35.4°, 43.2°, 57.0°, and 62.6°, which might be assigned to the reflections from the (220), (311), (400), (511) and (440) crystal planes of cubic phase of Fe3O4 NPs and the pragmatic peaks are reliable with standard JCPDS NO. 89-4319. Moreover, there is no noise of GQDs can be detected, which is explained by the small amounts, high dispersion and low crystalline of GQDs in GQD/Fe3O4 NC. Fig. 1(d) represented the powder XRD pattern for GQD/Fe3O4/Ppy NC, the observed sharp peaks demonstrated that the intensity of the diffraction peak of Ppy was decreased due to the effect of both Fe3O4 NPs and GQDs that obstructed the crystal plane of Ppy. Therefore, the XRD results confirmed the existence of both Ppy and GQDs composites on Fe3O4 spinal without damaging most of its crystal structure.

The interaction between the moieties and chemical structure of Ppy, Fe3O4, GQD/Fe3O4 NC and GQD/Fe3O4/Ppy NC was studied using FT-IR analysis (Fig. 2 ). The broad peak at 3420 cm−1 confirms the N-H asymmetric stretching in the pyrrole ring. The peaks at 1364 cm−1 demonstrate fundamental stretching vibrations of C-N in the Ppy ring while the peaks centred at 1100 cm−1 and 1044 cm−1 accredited to C-H stretching and bending vibrations indicate the presence of the Ppy backbone chain and as expected, in Fig. 2(a).The broad peak around between 3400 and 3500 cm−1 attributed to the NH asymmetric stretching vibration and water of crystallization found in Fig. 2 (c) & (d).

The short peak appeared at 2350 cm−1 attributed to N-H stretching of Ppy. The strong absorption peak present at 587 cm−1 is ascribed to Fe-O in Fe3O4 NPs as observed in Fig. 2(b). On the other hand, the small peak present at 933 cm−1 is due to the plane bending of C-H and the pyrrole ring (Fig. 2(d)). The peak intensity in the FTIR spectra of the GQD/Fe3O4 NC and GQD/Fe3O4/Ppy NC become remarkably weak after the composite formation, which indicated that Fe3O4 NPs might be decorated on the surface of the GQDs and Ppy. The possible reason is that the typical peaks of nanocomposites would decrease when other nanoparticles were decorated on the surfaces of GQDs and Ppy (Fig. 2c & d).

Furthermore, Fig. 3 displayed UV–Vis characterization of the Ppy (Black), Fe3O4 NPs (Red), GQD/Fe3O4 NC (Blue) and hybrid GQD/Fe3O4/Ppy NC (Pink). The UV–Vis absorption spectra of Ppy exhibits that the absorption peak located around 224 nm was recognized via the chain of the aromatic nuclei and corresponds to the π-π* transitions. It can be seen that the absorption intensity becomes stronger with a small red shift in these peaks positions after loading the GQDs and Ppy for Fe3O4. The results of the UV–Vis absorption study confirmed that the GQDs and Ppy in the Fe3O4 could increase the photon absorptive capacity of the sample and keep the inherent band gap of the semiconductor.

The surface morphology of Ppy, pristine Fe3O4 NPs and GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy NC was studied with the aid of FE-SEM. The FE-SEM image of Ppy revealed that the polymer is composed of highly aggregated cluster particles with the presence of little globules which are permeable and as depicted in Fig. 4 (a). Fig. 4(b) revealed the formation of spherical shaped Fe3O4 NPs with rough surfaces having non-agglomerated particle distribution with a mean particle size of 250.4 ± 54.3 nm. Finally, FE-SEM image of GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy NC displays a tiny sphere like Fe3O4 NPs seem to be blurred as they are homogeneously coated with a thin film of GQDs, which are randomly distributed and intensely covered by aggregated Ppy polymeric structure (Fig. 4(c)). Fig. 4(d–f) demonstrates the elemental compositions and purity of prepared samples via EDX analysis. EDX spectrum of Ppy (Fig. 4d) reveals the presence of C, N, O and S with weight percentages of 61.50%, 21.42%, 13.94 and 3.14% respectively, indicating successful synthesis of Ppy. Fig. 4(e) illustrates EDX analysis of the Fe3O4 NPs. It can be seen that the product is composed of 31.97%, and 68.03 wt% of oxygen and iron respectively. EDX spectrum of GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy NC (Fig. 4f) revealed the elemental composition of the nanocomposite is mainly C and O originating from GQDs, Fe3O4NPs and Ppy, Fe from Fe3O4 NPs, and N from Ppy.

The Elemental mapping images of Ppy, Fe3O4 NPs, GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy NC (Fig. 5 a–c) represent the uniform distribution of C, O, N, Fe, Cl elements throughout the samples corresponding to the molecular content of Ppy, Fe3O4 NPs and GQDs. The elemental mapping indicates the intense presence and distribution of carbon and oxygen than any other elements in the composite. This is due to their combined elemental contributions from GQDs, Fe3O4 NPs and Ppy.

The Morphological representation of GQDs was studied by HRTEM and displayed in Fig. 6 . TEM images (Fig. 6 (A-C) depict that the size distribution of the as-prepared GQDs was mainly possessed spherical morphology and the average size of the attained GQDs was about 3.3 nm. The crystalline structure of the GQDs is displayed in Fig. 6D. The measured lattice spacing is 0.15 nm, corresponding to the (001) lattice fringe of graphene. Fig. 6E illustrates the SAED pattern of GQDs.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy was carried out to comprehend the elemental analysis of the GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy NC, and Fig. 7 A represents the wide-scan spectra of GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy NC. The spectra illustrate the presence of C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, Cl 2p, and Fe 2p in the prepared nanocomposites. The N 1s peak located at 401.2 eV, referring to pyrrolic (C-N) nitrogen atoms [36,37]. The nitrogen obtained from Ppy could lead to the good electron conductivity and ion permeability of the Ppy polymeric structure, thus promoting the electrochemical performance [38]. A relatively high signal at 201.30 eV (Cl 2p) indicates the high chlorine content, which was ascribed to the introduction of oxidant FeCl3 for the polymerization of pyrrole, which could act as a dopant and be incorporated into the Ppy chains and is observed in the XPS and also EDX. The high-resolution Fe 2p spectrum represented in Fig. 7B and peak at 711.5 and 724.7 eV corresponds to the Fe3O4 (Fig. 7B) [39,40]. Further, Fig. 7C illustrates the enlarged scan of the O 1s spectrum, where the peak is located at binding energy positions of 532.7 eV, suggesting the presence of chemisorbed oxygen of the surface hydroxyls [41]. The deconvoluted C 1s spectrum is subdivided into three fragments at three different binding energies of 284.5, 286.4 and 288.9 eV are presented in Fig. 7D. The first two components are attributed to the presence of the carboxyl group bonding state and steady peak position π-π interactions in pyrrolic rings, respectively. The third component at 288.9 eV suggests the possible occurrence of C-S/C = O/C = N/=C-NH+ bonding structures in the nanocomposites (Fig. 7D). Of course, a major portion of the carbon and oxygen also comes from the GQDs and Fe3O4 NPs respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical activity of GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy modified GCE
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8 A displays the electrochemical impedance spectra for the different modified GCE at scanning frequencies from 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz. Rct values were calculated by Randles equivalent circuit. Rct Values for modified and unmodified GCE such as GQDs/GCE, Fe3O4/GCE, Ppy/GCE and GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy have been estimated around as 93.66, 90.9,100 and 76.91 Ω/cm2 respectively. From the observed Rct value of modified GCE is lower than that of the other pristine modified GCE.

Fig. 8B illustrated CV curves of different modified electrodes recorded in the 1 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−containing 1 M KCl solution system at the scan rate of 50 mV/s and the well-determined redox peaks was appeared. Compared with bare GCE (9.613 × 10−5), modified GCE such as Ppy/GCE (8.227 × 10−5), Fe3O4/GCE (6.771 × 10−6), GQDs/GCE (9.841 × 10−5), a considerable peak current was obtained at the modified GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy/GCE (1.073 × 10−4). The high peak current with a slight shift indicates GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy/GCE promote the fast electron transfer in the electrochemical process. These annotations aptly recommend that the GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy modified GCE is a new superficial electrode than the bare GCE and other pristine modified GCE electrodes. Fig. 8C demonstrates the CVs of GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy modified GCE recorded in the existence of above said condition with various scan rate from 10 to 200 mV/s. The redox peaks are well resolute and, it increase linearly while increasing the scan rate. The obtained results clearly suggest that the electrode reactions of FeCN investigated at the GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy/GCE are diffusion-controlled processes. Fig. 8D represents the corresponding linear plot for anodic and cathodic peak current vs. different scan rate. The active surface area of modified and unmodified GCE was determined by [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− redox system and active surface area was premeditated by using Randles-Sevcik equation [42],
(1) Ip = (2.69 × 105) n3/2 ACD1/2υ1/2
where A corresponds to the electrode area (cm2), D is the diffusion coefficient of [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− in solution, C represents the concentration of [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− (mol L−1), n is the number of electron and υ demonstrate the scan rate (mV/s). The active areas for Bare/GCE, GQDs/GCE, Fe3O4/GCE, Ppy/GCE and GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy/GCE were calculated as 0.08, 0.246, 0.72, 0.64 and 0.9 cm2 respectively. These findings demonstrate that the modified GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy/GCE has the largest active surface area, which greatly enhances the electrocatalytic activity for the detection of CTN. Furthermore, the rate constant for the modified and unmodified electrode was calculated by the following equation [43],
(2) Ket = RT/ F2Rct AC
where, A demonstrates the electrode surface area (cm2), Rct represents the charge resistance (Ω), F is the Faraday constant (96,485C mol−1), C is the concentration of [Fe (CN)6]3−/4− and R illustrate the universal rate constant (8.314 J K−1 mol−1). The heterogeneous rate constant for the modified and unmodified electrode was calculated as 4.59 × 10−4 and 0.42 × 10−4 cm s−1 respectively. Hence, the Ket is greater than unmodified GCE; it confirms a fast electron movement in this method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical investigation of CTN at GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy/GCE
ce:section.ce:para: Preliminarily, a cyclic voltammogram was used to inspect the electrochemical redox behaviour of CTN on various modified GCE in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) at a scan rate 50 mV/s. Fig. 9 A depicts the CV curves of bare GCE, GQD/GCE, Fe3O4/GCE, Ppy/GCE and GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy/GCE in the presence of 50 µM of CTN. Obviously, a well-defined, strong oxidation peak and wide reduction peak appeared for GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy/GCE when compared with other pristine modified electrodes. The GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy/GCE exhibited a significantly enhanced redox reaction and these results suggest that the GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy catalyze the reaction and enhance the oxidation of CTN through electron transfer at the electrode–electrolyte interface. Fig. 9B demonstrates the CVs curves recorded for GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy modified GCE at various scan rates in the presence of 50 µM of CTN under 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0). The obtained results exposed that the oxidation and reduction current simultaneously increased with an increase in their scan rate from the range of 100–500 mV/s, suggest that the electrochemical reaction is a diffusion-controlled process. Furthermore, Fig. 9B (Inset) represents the corresponding linear plots of redox peak current vs. various scan rate. Further, the possible mechanism for electrochemical reaction of CTN is displayed in Scheme 2 . Due to the presence of carbon and nitrogen in the Ppy matrix, pure Ppy typically has a low electrical conductivity. Also, GQDs are difficult to immobilize on the electrode surface, which restrict their utility in electrochemistry. After decorated with Fe3O4, it provides more active sites to enhance the significant electron transfer from CTN to its oxime form. Interestingly, when compared with pristine modified materials, the heterocyclic structure Ppy embedded with GQDs and Fe3O4 NC provides more active sites to enhance the significant electron transfer reaction from amine to hydroxylamine in the determination process of CTN.

The electrocatalytic behaviour of CTN was further analyzed by varying pH from 3.0 to 11.0 at 50 mV/s of scan rate and was depicted in Fig. 9C. From the Fig. 9C the oxidative peak current increases and peak potential shifted negatively while increasing the pH from 3.0 to 7.0 whereas, the peak current was decreased while increasing pH (above pH 7.0) because of the high accommodation and mobility of OH– ions on the electrode surface which causes electrostatic revulsion at electrode–electrolyte interface towards CTN. Experiments were repeated at equilibrium with pH values ranging from 7.2 to 7.8 to find the most suitable pH value. As displayed in Fig. 9D, the higher peak current was found at pH 7.0. Therefore, an electrolyte with a pH of 7.0 was used for the analysis of physiological samples. Additionally, the calibration plot illustrated the relationship between peak current and different pH (Fig. 9E).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of CTN at GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy modified GCE by DPV techniques
ce:section.ce:para: To estimate the feasibility of sensitive determination of CTN sensor based on GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy modified GCE was recorded by DPV. The experiential oxidative peak signals have been monitored by increasing the concentration of CTN by the standard addition method. The observed linear current signal depicts the electro-oxidations of CTN which could be linearly increased with their increasing of CTN between the concentrations 0.1 to 100 μM were shown in Fig. 10 A. The analogous linear association between the oxidation peak current ipa (μA) and increasing concentrations (μM) of CTN has been inferred as follows; Ipa (μA) = 0.011CCTN (μM) + 1.293 (R2 = 0.990), and the lowest detection limits (3σ/S) were calculated as 0.009 µM for CTN (Fig. 10B). GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy modified GCE provides the negatively shifted oxidative peak current for CTN which could be attributed due to its quantum efficient property and presence of Fe metal cations in nanocomposites which may form the metal-CTN complex in sensing progress and additionally GQDs/Fe3O4 NC embedded Ppy provides a large active site enabling with strong interaction towards the targeted CTN. In addition, GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy coated GCE displayed high signals for the oxidation of CTN due to the reason that the integrated metal oxides and GQDs embedded Ppy provide the large surface area in their composite formation which enhances the analytical performance and it can be able to detect the diffusion or migration process and to maintain the synergistic effect between nanocomposites to with held the high porosity and active sites in GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy in their structure. Moreover, the GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy material was low-cost, highly biocompatible, more flexible and easy to synthesize. These excellent smart features make the GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy modified GCE suitable for real-time application. The obtained results confirm that the presence of GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy drop casted GCE has achieved comparable remarks with other reports presented in Table 1 .

The size of the associated molecules and functional groups is important in determining the selectivity of the sensor for the target analyte. Generally, molecules with functional group composition and comparable size will prevent the detection of target molecules in the sample. Consequently, the system was evaluated through a common interference at physiological concentration of Uric acid (UA), Urea (U), glucose (Glu), ascorbic acid (AA), L-Cysteine (L-Cys), and selective inorganic ions Pb2+ and Cd2+, which was 20-fold greater concentration than CTN. The findings illustrated in Fig. 10C, indicate that the detection technique is extremely specific to CTN alone and free of interference. As depicted in Scheme 2, a strong synergistic combination effect between GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy and CTN induces an advantageous electro catalytic environment in which the aromatic CTN molecules approaches the electrode surface with ease through electrostatic attraction. These findings specify that the GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy sensor can be suggested to sense the CTN directly in their multifaceted mixture in extremely minimal concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility, repeatability, cyclic stability and storage capability of developed sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The reproducibility of the proposed sensor was done with DPV with 100 µM consecutive addition of CTN in 0.1 M PBS with three different modified GCE and the results are displayed in Fig. S1A. The facts that all the three electrodes demonstrated the proposed sensor have superior reproducibility with relative standard deviation (RSD) value of 2.93%. Additionally, the proposed GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy NC exhibits very good repeatability with a relative standard deviation (RSD) value of 3.54%, as can be seen in Fig. S1B. Further, the stability of the modified GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy electrode was evaluated with different period and observed bar graph represented in Fig. S1C. Particularly, 96.78% of the initial peak current response was retained after the use for 10 days use, which illustrates the excellent stability of the modified electrode. Besides, continuous CV tests towards CTN and the first and 100th cycles represented in Fig. S1D were used to study the cycling stability of the proposed sensor. Following the analysis of the cycling stability, the sensing performance was observed to be 97.51% stable after 100 cycles. The results that were observed following the cycling stability are demonstrated in Fig. S1E, which attests to superiority of the suggested sensor.

Real-time biological fluid sample analysis is crucial for clinical diagnosis. Human blood serum and urine samples were measured for CTN using GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy/GCE, allowing studies to test the practicality of the fabricated sensor. The urine and blood serum samples were picked up from the KIDNEY CENTRE, Madurai. The accuracy of the finding of CTN by standard addition method was ascertained via DPV. As a result, diluted solution was spiked with known concentration of CTN, and these solutions were utilized to analysis the actual samples. Fig. 11 A&B details the DPV results for the detection of CTN in human urine and serum samples at concentrations ranging from 5 to 40 µM. The injected values and results were measured by utilizing standard addition method (Table S1). Observed oxidation peaks were highly confirms and suggest, the fabricated sensor device can be utilized for real time CTN sensors in clinical applications.

Further the DPV method was carried out on the modified GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy/GCE in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) to test the real sample analysis on live urine and blood serum samples without adding synthetic CTN. After that, the addition of both urine sample (100 µL–1000 µL) and blood serum (100 µL − 320 µL) were directly and separately injected into the supporting electrolyte. A wide peak with a negative shift was observed, which may be different from commercial synthetic sample peaks due to the presence of natural CTN present in the human biological samples (Fig. 11C&D). Particular peak shift may be possible due to that the patient under medication. When we go for real time sample it is not possible to get a sample from patients without medication. Moreover, the same peaks were precisely increases while adding the repeated content of both urine and blood serum samples in the same cell. Then, the actual amount of the CTN in the human urine was calculated to be just about approximately 90 µM for 1000 µL in whole urine content. From the peak we can predict and suggest that the patient is suffering with renal failure and the detection of high creatinine may be predicted by fabricated sensor. These results obviously confirmed and comment, the fabricated sensor can be utilized for real time CTN sensors in clinical applications and the newly designed sensor is an accurate platform to detect CTN in blood serum and urine samples.

In this work, GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy nanocomposite were successfully synthesized via a reliable solvothermal method at low cost. Synthesized GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy NC were adapted to sense the biological potential compound CTN by the electrochemical DPV method. Modified catalyst such that GQDs/Fe3O4/Ppy/GCE displayed good selectivity and high sensitivity towards the detection of CTN with the low detection limits of 0.009 μM over the wide linear range from 0.1 to 100 μM. Furthermore, the practical application of the developed sensor device was used to analyse the real human blood serum and urine samples with high accuracy and satisfactory recovery level for the determination of CTN. From these results, here we concluded our fabricated electrochemical sensor device has capability of monitoring the CTN in the clinical test samples for human health benefits.

Chandran Bhuvaneswari: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis. Arumugam Elangovan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Narayanan Sudhan: Conceptualization, Methodology. Ganesan Vinodhkumar: Conceptualization, Methodology. Soorangkattan Saravanan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis. Venkataraman Balasubramanian: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis. Chandrasekaran Sharmila: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis. Sudha Karuppaiah: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Dr. A. E expresses gratitude to The Management of Thiagarajar College, and DST, Govt of India, (DST-SB/EMEQ)- 215/2013, UGC, Govt of India (UGC-MRP 6451/16(SERO/UGC)) for their financial support. Dr. AE thanks and is grateful to the Madurai Kidney Centre for providing Human blood serum and Urine samples for Creatinine analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.109259.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
